- d) Connection to continuous service

The location, address, and means of connection to the continuous service responsible for winter maintenance performance shall be stated.

- e) List of senior employees ensuring winter maintenance

All senior employees of the road administrator, roadmasters, and foremen in roadmaster's districts shall be listed as follows:

Name and surname, function, workplace, workplace telephone, home telephone.

- f) List of spreading material storage facilities:

Location, type of material, capacity, estimated stock level as of November 1.

- g) Agreed assistance

Company (organization), registered office, operator's name, telephone, agreed activity.

- h) Winter maintenance regime in protected landscape areas and in drinking water source areas, in protective zones of natural healing sources and natural sources of table mineral waters, on bridges according to recommended ČSN 73 6221

Section name - road number, local name, maintenance technology.

- i) Documents

- records from winter maintenance staff meetings

- agreements on mutual assistance in winter maintenance on roads crossing from neighboring territories, including agreements on technologies used

- approval clause of the Ministry of Transport and Communications

- agreements on assistance concluded according to letter g)

- other documents.

- 3. Winter maintenance plan for local roads

Decisions on the preparation of the local road winter maintenance plan and its scope are made by municipalities according to § 42 (2) of the decree.

- 4. Winter maintenance operational staffs

- 4.1. Auxiliary bodies of road administrators are district operational staffs for road winter maintenance. The road administrator shall invite representatives of the transport department and environmental department of the District Office, the district directorate of the Police of the Czech Republic, and legal entities that significantly contribute to material and technical cooperation in ensuring road winter maintenance, to participate in the operational staff.

Operational staffs for winter maintenance are established and their meetings convened by the road and motorway administrator.

- 4.2. The Ministry of Transport and Communications establishes the main operational staff for road and motorway winter maintenance as its auxiliary body for the purpose of operational resolution of extraordinary situations. The Ministry of Transport and Communications shall invite, in particular, representatives of the Police Presidium of the Czech Republic, the Ministry of Defence, and the Ministry of the Environment to participate in the operational staff.

- 4.3. At its meeting before the start of the winter period, the operational staff shall discuss the winter maintenance plan, its organization, and mutual connections. Further meetings are convened operationally as needed. In cases where the road or motorway administrator is unable to manage an emergency situation arising from extraordinary weather conditions, even with the deployment of all its own and all available forces and resources in the area, the operational staff shall be convened. In entirely extraordinary weather situations that threaten the life and health of citizens, or in cases of imminent major property damage, district authorities shall exercise powers according to § 5 (2) to (4) of Act No. 425/1990 Sb., as amended.

- 4.4. Municipalities shall proceed according to point 3; in the event of a state of emergency according to point 4.3., including the use of powers according to § 47 of Act No. 367/1990 Sb., as amended.

- 5. Reporting

Motorway and road administrators shall submit information on the passability of these communications to the workplace authorized by the Ministry of Transport and Communications at specified deadlines, which ensures informing the public through media.

sprilohac7kvyhlas.text:
WINTER MAINTENANCE TECHNOLOGY

- 1. Measures before commencing winter maintenance

In preparation for winter maintenance, road and highway administrators shall analyze the past winter period and ensure, in particular:

- readiness of mechanisms for winter maintenance, verification of knowledge of workers performing winter maintenance (technology, regulations for operating mechanisms, work safety rules, etc.) by October 15

- negotiation of assistance agreements by October 31

- negotiation of access to adjacent land and erection of snow fences by November 30.

- a) Erection of snow fences

In places of repeated danger of road blockage by snow, snow fences are placed at a safe distance from the edge of the road crown on the windward side, if possible perpendicular to the direction of prevailing winds, parallel to the road at a distance of 12 - 18 times their height, depending on local conditions. When the snowdrift and snow accumulation reach the full height of the snow fences and the snow surface then completely levels above their upper edge, another row of snow fences shall be erected in front of the first row against the prevailing wind direction at a distance of 12 to 25 meters, depending on the depth of the cut.

The road administrator shall proceed with the placement of snow fences in such a way as to avoid damage to the affected land and to ensure that the snow fences are sufficiently secured against the effects of wind.

- b) Orientation snow poles

In areas with excessive snowfall, orientation snow poles are installed to improve orientation during plowing. Poles equipped with reflectors or painted black and yellow have a diameter of approximately 5 cm. Their length is 1 - 3 m depending on the height of the snow cover in the given area. They are installed 50 cm outside the cleared area at distances of approximately 50 m. This distance is appropriately shortened in curves or in places with frequent fog.

- c) Marking of unmaintained roads

Roads whose passability is not ensured during the winter period must be marked before the start of the winter period with traffic sign A 22 „Other danger“ with an additional sign „Road not maintained in winter“.

- d) Marking of technology change

Where there is a change in the gritting technology on a single road route, traffic sign A 22 „Other danger“ must be installed with an additional sign, e.g., „End of chemical gritting“.

- e) Conclusion of agreements

The road administrator shall conclude agreements on assistance in emergency situations, agreements on mutual exchange of maintained roads, and agreements on uniform maintenance of roads passing through the territories of multiple administrators sufficiently in advance, so that their passability is ensured, if possible, using the same technology. If no agreement is reached or if it is not technically possible, the technology used must be approved by the road administrative authorities. Places of technology change must be marked according to paragraph d).

- f) Training of persons performing winter maintenance

All persons performing winter maintenance must be properly and demonstrably trained (including persons performing winter maintenance on a contractual basis). The training curriculum must include, in addition to technical and organizational instructions, rules for safe work in winter maintenance and principles of environmental protection.

- 2. Mechanical snow removal

Snow removal is primarily carried out mechanically. Applying grit or spraying chemical de-icing materials onto a layer of freshly fallen snow higher than 3 cm without prior plowing is ineffective and therefore impermissible. In typical winter conditions, snow must be removed to prevent it from being compacted by traffic and freezing to the road surface. Slush must be removed from the road.

Side snow banks must be widened to maintain clearance and prevent potential obstruction of visibility. During thawing, water drainage must be ensured.

On two-lane, two-way roads, snow is cleared from the center of the carriageway to the right edge of the road. In case of extraordinary snowfall, in through sections and on local roads, snow is only pushed to the edges of sidewalks and, if possible, removed.

Snow removal by driving against the flow of traffic is permissible only in exceptional cases and under specified safety measures.

On multi-lane roads, it is advisable to clear snow in multi-member work teams.

At rest areas and parking lots, snow may first be pushed into banks, which are then removed.

At level crossings of roads with a retained snow layer and railways, care must be taken to ensure that this snow layer smoothly connects to the railway crossing's grade. At underpasses, care must be taken to maintain free height.

On bridges, snow is removed from the entire width and length of the bridge. Snow should be moved longitudinally if possible or removed if, when thrown to the sides, it would fall onto underlying traffic routes or other objects.

Snow plows are commonly used for snow removal. If snowdrifts occur during snowfall or if the snow height reaches approximately 30 - 50 cm, V-plows are deployed. Snow layers higher than 70 cm are removed by snow blowers. Snow throwers are used to widen the carriageway clearance.

During continuous snowfall, plowing is repeated periodically. On traffic-important roads, the residual snow layer should not exceed 3 cm after plowing is completed.

- 3. Snow removal using chemical de-icing materials

This technology is used only on roads designated by the winter service plan. Salt gritting begins if the snow height does not exceed 3 cm. Gritting is not permitted on snow layers higher than 3 cm.

Dosing during gritting with sodium chloride or calcium chloride is carried out depending on the intensity of snowfall. At low intensity (1 - 1.5 cm per hour), a dose of 10 g.m^-2 is applied; at higher intensity, a dose of 20 g.m^-2. The use of larger doses already causes the formation of slush. In case of exceptionally long snowfall or extraordinary intensity of precipitation, gritting with a dose of 10 g.m^-2 can be repeated during snowfall, but always only after plowing has been carried out, so that the salt reaches the road surface.

- 4. Removal of black ice or compacted snow layers using chemical de-icing materials

Chemical de-icing materials are fundamentally applied only to the residual snow layer that can no longer be removed or reduced by mechanical means. These materials can effectively and quickly remove compacted snow layers up to 1 - 2 cm thick or black ice up to 2 mm thick. For thicker layers, repeated gritting and the cooperation of mechanical means are necessary. At temperatures around -5 °C, sodium chloride is used; at temperatures around -15 °C, calcium chloride is used – in the intermediate temperature range, a mixture of both chlorides may be used. The dosing specified below applies to both sodium chloride and calcium chloride, and the stated maximum doses must not be exceeded.

 The minimum, technologically sufficient dose for this type of gritting is 20 g.m^-2. When eliminating thicker layers of black ice, it is generally necessary to use higher doses; however, the total consumption of gritting salts should not exceed 60 g.m^-2 per intervention day. Only in entirely exceptional cases, when it is essential to quickly restore full road passability, can a dose higher than 60 g.m^-2 be used. Doses higher than 20 g.m^-2 must not be used on road sections exposed to strong winds, unless a wetting device is used on the gritter.

For thin layers of black ice, the grit is left to act for approximately 2 hours. For thicker layers of black ice, the grit is left to act for 2 - 5 hours. If the layer does not completely detach from the surface within this time, the loosened surface layer is plowed with a steel-bladed plow, and the entire procedure is repeated. The total consumption of gritting salt must not exceed the permitted doses mentioned above.

Any resulting slush or ice chips must be mechanically removed from the road immediately.

Only in extraordinary cases (during freezing rain or freezing drizzle) can a mixture of abrasive and chemical material be exceptionally used for gritting. The dose is chosen such that the total amount of the chemical component does not exceed the maximum dose mentioned above – e.g., a maximum of 240 g.m^-2 of a mixture with a ratio of 1 : 5.

- 5. Roughening of black ice or compacted snow layers by gritting with abrasive materials

Gritting with abrasive materials is used on roads that are not maintained using chemical de-icing materials. Only occasional gritting may be carried out at traffic-important locations or where the traffic-technical condition of the road requires it (intersections, steep ascents, sharp directional curves, public transport stops). At these locations, it is advisable to establish self-service depots for abrasive materials.

Fine-grained material (grains smaller than 2 mm) is suitable for gritting black ice. For gritting non-iced compacted snow layers, material with a higher content of coarse fractions (grains larger than 4 mm) should be used.

In built-up areas, material with grains larger than 8 mm must not be used.

Straight road sections are gritted with a dose of approximately 70 to 100 g.m^-2; places where the traffic-technical condition of the road requires it are gritted with a dose of approximately 300 g.m^-2. On busy icy roads, it is advisable to increase doses by 50 to 100% due to material being swept away by traffic. However, doses higher than 500 g.m^-2 are inefficient.

Gritting with abrasive materials is carried out across the entire width of the road, or across the entire width of the traffic lane, i.e., at least 3.5 m in one direction.

- 6. Snow and ice removal on local roads of class IV

Municipalities decide on snow and ice removal on local roads of class IV to ensure their passability. The passability of these roads is ensured by sweeping or plowing snow, scraping frozen deposits, and gritting with abrasive materials. Only sidewalks and paths where no utility networks are laid (unless they have a closed cover) and which are separated from green areas and tree strips in such a way that salty solution cannot drain onto them may be gritted with chemical de-icing material.

- 7. Mechanisms for winter maintenance

- a) Spreaders for chemical de-icing materials must meet the following technical requirements:

- the dose of chemical de-icing material in g.m^-2 on the desired width of the lane should be adjustable within the range of 10 - 60 g.m^-2, preferably continuously,

- for stepped regulation of the spreading dose, the possibility of setting 10, 20, 30, 40, and 60 g.m^-2 is required, but at least 10, 20, and 40 g.m^-2

- the set dosage should be automatically maintained even with changes in the spreader's working speed within the range of 10 to 60 km.h^-1

- the spreading width should be adjustable at least within the range of 2 - 9 m; for spreaders with a constant spreading width, it should be at least 2 m, maximum 3.5 m

- the transverse and longitudinal uniformity of spreading, depending on the quality of the gritting salt, should be stated in the spreader's technical specifications

- spreaders equipped with a wetting device should allow for maintaining a ratio of dry material to brine of 7 : 3 when dosing 5 - 20 g.m^-2. The recommended concentration of sodium chloride in brine is 18 - 21 %. Before the winter period, the actual dosage in g.m^-1 with the material used must be checked, and the setting of the dosing mechanism corrected according to the results, so that the actual doses are in line with the required values.

- b) Spreaders for abrasive materials must meet the following technical requirements:

- the dose of abrasive gritting material should be adjustable within the range of 70 - 500 g.m^-2 (at least up to 300 g.m^-2), which corresponds to 0.05 - 0.33 l.m^-2 of aggregate at a bulk density of 1500 kg.m^-3

- for stepped regulation of the gritting dose, the possibility of setting doses of 70, 100, 200, 300 (500) g.m^-2 when using abrasive materials with a grain size of max. 18 mm is required, but continuous adjustment is preferred

- the set dose should be automatically maintained even with changes in the spreader's working speed within the range of 10 - 40 km.h^-1

- the spreading width (for spreaders with adjustable spreading width) should be adjustable within the range of 2.5 - 10 m (minimum 2.4 - 5 m)

- the spreading width for spreaders with a constant width should be within the range of 2.5 to 3.5 m

- the transverse and longitudinal non-uniformity of spreading, depending on the granulation of the gritting material, should be stated in the spreader's technical specifications.

- 8. Recommended materials for winter maintenance

- a) Chemical de-icing materials

The following chemical de-icing materials may be used for gritting roads, highways, and local roads, with the exception of non-motorized roads:

- Sodium chloride

- Calcium chloride

- Mixtures of chlorides

**Sodium chloride** - (rock salt, table salt) NaCl - is effective for removing black ice and snow layers at temperatures down to -5 °C and, under certain conditions, even lower.

**Calcium chloride**, CaCl2 - is effective for removing black ice and snow layers below -15 °C.

**Quality of gritting salts**

Used gritting salts should not contain more than 5 weight percent of dust particles smaller than 0.16 mm. The grain size distribution is significant for the method of use and gritting technique. Fine particles cause rapid surface dissolution, but the depth effect is limited. Coarse particles penetrate deeper, so compacted snow layers or thicker black ice are broken up by the effect of road traffic.

Salt should not show more than 2 weight percent of stable moisture upon delivery and should contain at least 96 weight percent of effective dissolving substance.

Upon delivery of salt, data on chemical composition, proportion of insoluble impurities, moisture, and grain size distribution must be documented in the following breakdown:

below 0.16 mm

from 0.16 mm to 0.80 mm

from 0.80 mm to 3.15 mm

from 3.15 mm to 5.00 mm

above 5.00 mm

Limits for the content of heavy metals and unsuitable impurities have not yet been established by special regulations.

**Storage of chemical de-icing materials**

Chemical de-icing materials are not permitted to be stored in open dumps. The most efficient storage options are all-wood halls with a capacity of 500 - 1000 t or all-wood silos with a capacity of 40 - 200 t, positioned with regard to the possibilities of filling spreaders. In enclosed warehouses, leakage into the surroundings and seepage into the subsoil must be prevented. In an enclosed warehouse, loose sodium chloride may be stored.

**Solutions of de-icing agents**

Solutions of de-icing agents with a concentration of 18 - 21 % are used for wetting gritting salts. Mobile mixing stations are designated for preparing the solution (brine).

Direct use of de-icing agent solutions for removing black ice or compacted snow layers is possible if temperatures are not expected to drop below -3 °C. Solutions are applied by sprinklers that allow for uniform spraying. When determining the limit doses of solutions, the concentration of the active substance is taken as a basis, the amount of which must not exceed the limit dose values according to point 3.

- b) Abrasive gritting materials

From an environmental perspective, clean materials – sands and crushed aggregates – are preferred. From an economic perspective, bulk materials from local sources, such as slag and clinker, can be used. The slags and clinkers used must not contain toxic or otherwise harmful substances, should have appropriate granulation, and their grains should be angular. The harmlessness of these materials must be attested annually by their supplier. Gritting materials must not contain clay particles – sand should be sharp, hard, and free of larger grains.

The grain size of abrasive gritting materials should be in the range of 0.5 to 8 mm. In no case should abrasive material contain particles smaller than 0.3 mm or larger than 16 mm.

Storage of abrasive gritting materials

Abrasive materials can be stored in open spaces, but storage in silos, halls, or under shelters is more suitable. Provided that the storage facility for this material is constructed to prevent water seepage into the surroundings and subsoil, a small dose (1 - 3 %) of sodium chloride can be used to prevent the piles from freezing.

- c) Mixtures of abrasive and chemical de-icing materials

Due to a number of negative properties, the use of mixed materials is permissible only in extraordinary circumstances where gritting with these materials individually fails. For the preparation of mixed material, suitable sand or crushed aggregate is used, into which sodium chloride is uniformly mixed in a ratio of 1 part chloride to 3 to 6 parts sand.

The maximum permissible dose of this gritting per single application is derived from the amount of chloride in the mixture.

When using a mixture in a 1 : 6 ratio, it is 280 g.m^-2; when using a mixture in a 1 : 3 ratio, it is only 160 g.m^-2.

DAILY RECORD OF WINTER MAINTENANCE AND ITS EVALUATION

Administrators of motorways and roads keep winter maintenance documentation as a separate logbook, in which they chronologically record all findings and activities in such a way that nothing can be subsequently added to or omitted from the logbook.

During service, all reports, notifications, communications, deployments for inspection or intervention, returns, road condition reports, weather, sudden temperature changes, precipitation, wind, reports submitted to central or regional dispatch, and reported traffic accidents that are presumed to have been caused by a defect in road condition, as well as records of inspections by senior employees or control bodies, etc., are recorded.

At the end of service, the passability or impassability of communications is recorded, and the correctness of the logbook is confirmed by the signature of the responsible employee. Records are made chronologically in sequence so that no additional data can be subsequently inserted. The entry is made with one carbon copy. Completed logbooks are archived for five years.

- 1. Winter Maintenance Logbook for Motorways and Roads

Shift supervisor (for roads also shift foreman and district shift foreman)

- name, surname

Shift workers

- list of employees for winter maintenance according to actual presence

On-call duty at home

- list of employees to reinforce the shift

Vehicles and machinery in operation

- numbers and types of machinery allocated for winter maintenance operations in operational condition for the given shift

Weather situation

- temperature, clear, partly cloudy, overcast, rain, fog, snowfall, wind, etc.

Road condition

- brief description (for roads according to order of importance), road passable, passability limited, snow layer in cm, black ice, snowdrifts in km, road impassable - traffic diverted - expected passability

Course of service

- time of start and end of service

- chronological record of the most important events

- record of road condition inspection

- road condition reports from other authorities - time, content, and who provided the information are stated

- notification of defects carried over to the next shift.

- 2. Winter Maintenance Logbook for Local Communications

For persons ensuring the passability of local communications through winter maintenance in the territory of the Capital City of Prague, Pilsen, Brno, and Ostrava, keeping a logbook according to point 1. is mandatory; for other municipalities, it is recommended.

- 3. Evaluation of Winter Maintenance for Motorways and Roads

The evaluation of winter maintenance is compiled using the following basic data:

- extent of the maintained network

- assessment of preparation for the winter period in relation to the actual course of winter maintenance

- assessment of mechanical equipment, number of machines, their failure rate, repairs, etc.

- evaluation of used technologies, their adherence, and inspection results

- assessment of work organization - personnel provision of tasks, adherence to time limits, etc.

- assessment of other conditions - location and social facilities of workplaces, assistance, etc.
